data.title: Building An Income Portfolio? 3 Picks To Start With For An Average Yield Of 6%+
data.description:
Introduction

As a dividend-focused investor, I always think about if I were starting fresh today, what stocks I would put in my portfolio. I also think about companies that would be great for retirees.

As a non-traditional retiree, I've developed a soft spot for retirees of the traditional retirement age. For years I've been trying to get my uncle to let me help him build a retirement portfolio.

I actually spoke with him recently, and he said he plans to retire this January when he turns 67. But he's been saying this for years, and I wouldn't be surprised if he continues to work.

Although I don't particularly know his financial situation, I know he's had a financial advisor. And he admitted during our conversation he doesn't know what he has in his portfolio. And that he needed to learn more, but I know investing can be intimidating for some.

In this article, I discuss what 3 stocks I would start with if I were building an income-focused portfolio for myself or for a retiree of the traditional retirement age.

The Power of Dividends

Although there's been chatter here on SA about how dividends don't matter, I've never believed that story. Yes, as an income-focused investor, you're subject to double taxation. Especially if you don't own a Roth IRA and are not over the age of 59 1/2.

But for those who are, dividends are a great way to retire early. In the future, I believe the retirement age will continue to increase. The traditional retirement age used to be 62 to receive full benefits.

But now this has increased to the age of 66 1/2. When I retired from the military in 2023, I knew I was taking a pay cut. For service members with 20 years of service, you receive 50% of your base pay for the rest of your life.

Not only does your paycheck shrink, but you also go from getting paid twice a month to once. For some, this can be scary. Especially those with a lot of household debt. Fortunately, I was smart enough to not have much revolving (debt).

So, I wasn't forced to go out and find a traditional job and continue working. Of course I kick myself for not investing much earlier. But better late than never. And income investing is something I'm trying to instill in my friends & family.

Here's a chart of dividend contributions to the overall market from 1960 until 2024. For many, dividend investing is boring because it's a slow process. Nowadays, people are looking for instant gratification or get-rich-quick schemes.

In my opinion, to be a successful dividend investor, you need to have at least a 10-year outlook at minimum if you want to build a solid income stream from quality dividend-paying stocks.

As a "quality over quantity" type of guy, I look to buy and hold companies that not only will likely be around for another 10 to 20 years but will continue to pay and grow their dividends. And these three companies I discuss today I expect to do just that.

#1 Realty Income Corporation (O) - 5.58% Yield

First on the list is everyone's favorite monthly-paying REIT, Realty Income. I covered O last month, assigning the REIT a Hold rating. This had less to do with their fundamentals but more so to do with limited upside. And also, I think Agree Realty (ADC) is better for the long term due to higher expected growth.

But from a valuation standpoint, Realty Income is the better buy at the moment with a forward P/AFFO multiple of 13.34x compared to 17.42x for ADC at the time of writing. So, if you're looking to build an income portfolio, O makes more sense because of the significantly cheaper price.

While I don't expect them to see as much growth as ADC going forward, I do expect them to continue paying and raising the monthly dividend for the foreseeable future. And as a retiree, this is likely your main goal. So, if my uncle wanted me to help him build a portfolio today, I would suggest Realty Income.

During their latest earnings, O saw accelerated investment activity in Europe. The REIT invested $1.4 billion, with only $380 million in the U.S., at a yield of 7%.

In Europe they saw a slightly higher yield of 7.7%. As a result of this and a 10 basis point increase in occupancy to 98.7%, AFFO climbed $0.03 from the previous quarter to $1.08.

And although growth for 2025 is disappointing compared to the 4.8% they saw in 2024, 2.16% AFFO growth is better than none. But same-store growth for the year is expected to be a disappointing 1%.

But I'm confident in the company's ability to maintain a safe & reliable dividend for the foreseeable future. Their AFFO payout ratio through 3 quarters stood at 75.5%, up from 74.4% the prior year.

The REIT also entered into a new business vertical of private capital. This gives O diversified sources of income, positioning them to likely see solid growth going forward.

The balance sheet was also solid, with a net debt to EBITDA of 5.4x. They also have an A credit rating with plenty of liquidity and capital sources to satisfy their upcoming debt of $1.1 billion. Total liquidity stood at $3.5 billion.

#2 VICI Properties Inc. (VICI) - 6.06% Yield

VICI Properties, like Realty Income, gets a lot of love here on Seeking Alpha. Although some say the stock has been a disappointing investment, I think this depends on your philosophy and cost basis.

VICI has pulled back recently due to headwinds in Las Vegas and risks from their tenant, Caesars Entertainment (CZR). Caesar's is VICI's largest tenant, accounting for 39% of rent roll.

The city has seen declining foot traffic for double-digit months due to financially constrained consumers as a result of higher-for-longer interest rates.

As someone who frequently visits, paying $9 for a bottle of water is a turnoff for many. Throw in expensive resort & parking fees, and I'm not surprised by the traffic slowdown.

But I do expect things will likely pick back up sometime next year as interest rates are likely to continue coming down. And this will alleviate pressure for consumers. But even with the slowdown, VICI continues to navigate successfully.

Moreover, the casino operators are likely to continue paying rent, as evidenced by VICI's 100% rent collection during COVID. And during their latest quarter, VICI showed resilience amidst uncertainty. VICI also continues to diversify outside of the Las Vegas Strip.

Revenue grew from $964.7 million to more than $1 billion. AFFO was flat at $0.60 from the prior quarter. But managed to see solid growth of 5.3% year-over-year. VICI also entered into a deal with Golden Entertainment to acquire 7 assets, mostly regional casinos in Nevada.

Additionally, management raised full-year guidance for the third time this year, a testament to their fundamentals. AFFO is now expected in a range of $2.36 - $2.37, representing a growth rate of 4.6% from 2024.

I've also liked VICI below $30, and currently the stock sits at $28.88. This puts their forward P/AFFO multiple at 12.21x, even lower than Realty Income's. Moreover, VICI ended the quarter with a solid leverage ratio of 5.2x, down from 5.4x the year prior.

They have more than $1 billion in debt due in 2026. But liquidity was solid at $3.1 billion.

Their payout ratio was also solid at roughly 75%. This gives them ample retained capital for internal growth and room for future dividend increases. In September, VICI raised their dividend for the 8th consecutive year. And I see this continuing for the foreseeable future.

#3 Verizon Communications Inc. (VZ) - 6.62% Yield

Lastly, Verizon is the 3rd stock I would place in the income-focused portfolio. And this is because in recent months the stock has pulled back, making them much more attractive. I've always liked adding to Verizon around or below the $40 level.

The stock currently sits at $40.70. And pulled back from around $45 in August. This is likely due to economic uncertainty and the new hire of PayPal's (PYPL) former CEO, Dan Schulman. I also discussed Verizon's latest earnings last month.

The company has slowly been seeing improvements. Management has managed to reduce debt, with leverage declining quarter-over-quarter from 2.3x to 2.2x. Of the big three telecom companies, VZ actually has the lowest leverage ratio.

T-Mobile's (TMUS) stood at 2.5x, while AT&T's (T) was slightly higher at 2.59x. And all three stand to benefit from lower interest rates since they're known for high debt loads. During the quarter, EPS beat estimates by $0.03, amounting to $1.21. This increased 1.6%, while revenue grew 1.5%.

Nothing exciting, and a very Verizon-like quarter. It's no secret that VZ has lagged behind peers, particularly TMUS, when it comes to subscriber growth. But their new CEO looks to change this going forward.

He has been aggressively making changes with store closures and job cuts as part of the company's restructuring. Me, personally, I think this is what Verizon has been missing, as their old CEO seemed a bit passive to me. While this will take at least a year or two to see benefits, I think it's good if VZ wants to compete with peers.

Verizon is also looking to leverage AI, positioning the company for long-term success. This not only will allow them to see solid earnings growth but also ensure that the dividend remains safe for the foreseeable future.

VZ's payout ratio was a conservative 54.4%. This gives them retained capital for growth and to potentially implement a buyback program. And I think this could drive earnings growth and price appreciation. But I don't see this happening for at least another year or two.

From a valuation standpoint, VZ trades at a single-digit multiple. And if their new CEO continues to be aggressive and can successfully execute, I think solid upside over the next year or two is likely.

Why These Three?

Realty Income, VICI Properties, and Verizon all trade at attractive valuations compared to their peers. And although they could trade rangebound and see limited upside over the near term, all three will likely see tailwinds from lower base rates.

Moreover, their fundamentals remain solid, and they are likely to deliver reliable income for the foreseeable future despite uncertain economic conditions. All three also offer double-digit upside from their current prices.

Realty Income:

VICI Properties:

Verizon:

Risks & Investor Takeaway

All three companies are somewhat sensitive to interest rates. Although rates are expected to decline this month, going forward is still uncertain.

Additionally, I think the economy could continue to see rises in unemployment. And while all three are fundamentally sound, sour market sentiment could result in limited upside until there's more economic clarity.

But for income-oriented investors, all three have attractive valuations compared to peers, yields near or above 6%, and well-covered dividends. So, if I were building an all-income portfolio from scratch today, these three are ones I would consider.

data.title: 2026 S&P 500 Outlook: The Start Of Natural Selection In AI
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I reiterate the recommendation to buy assets that track the S&P 500 index. This article is a special edition of my weekly series starting November 7, 2025, where I bring valuable insights about investments and the American economy.

In this article, we will cover the outlook for 2026. The article will cover topics such as results, expectations for the artificial intelligence market, and robust data to estimate the fair value of the S&P 500 at the end of 2026.

Context

More than 95% of S&P 500 companies have released Q3 results. 83% of companies reported EPS above expectations, and 76% reported revenue above expectations. It caught my attention that we are in the fourth consecutive quarter of double-digit earnings growth, and for reference, Q3 earnings growth was 13.4%.

Better than analyzing past data is projecting the future. In this sense, we have excellent prospects for 2026, as most sectors indicate more positive revisions than negative ones in guidances, as we can see below. Healthcare, technology, and financials draw positive attention, while consumption and energy draw negative attention.

This is What You Should Watch Out for in 2026

In the midst of the data reported above, we should have an unusual situation in 2026: competition in artificial intelligence, and curiously, while I was writing this article, the SA published the Wall Street Breakfast with the following call: Sam Altman Declares Code Red.

Basically, the post indicates that OpenAI should increase efforts on ChatGPT customizations as competition is increasing. In my opinion, 2026 will mark the beginning of natural selection in the AI ​​segment, or the transition from blue ocean to red ocean.

ChatGPT has 800 million weekly users, which guarantees a break for Gemini, which has 650 million monthly users; however, Gemini had 450 million users in July, meaning it is robust growth.

The market understood this, and everything is reflected in prices, as we can see in the graph below. In my opinion, competition should not be restricted to just generative AI models, as we will also have competition in other segments. Just look at Google's recent advances with its TPUs, which increase the competitive environment for Nvidia's GPUs.

In this scenario, the investor starts to ask questions like, Which stock to choose, Nvidia or Alphabet? In my opinion, that is not the point! In fact, my recommended stock portfolio for 2025 includes Nvidia and Alphabet shares, and the tendency is for both to be maintained in 2025. Year to date, the portfolio rises 26%, while the ETF SPY rises 16%.

I do not intend to expand on the explanations regarding the companies' theses, as this will be the subject of an article in the future; however, clearly Nvidia continues to be a pioneer in launching innovations in AI, and the hyperscalers must consolidate themselves by launching specific competitors for each technology, such as the dispute between GPUs (Nvidia) and TPUs (Alphabet).

I think Nvidia will continue to be a pioneer in the artificial intelligence market and will launch robust and versatile technologies. Meanwhile, other companies will compete occasionally with proprietary, specific, and lower-cost technologies.

But like I said, that's not the point! The point is that we will see the start of natural selection in AI next year, and increased competition will bring a phenomenon seen in several other markets: prices of services and products will tend to reduce, and this could positively impact several other sectors! The most curious thing is that this process has already started, in my opinion.

As a reference, in Q3 Magnificent Seven showed earnings growth of 18.4%; however, this growth is below the average of 28.8% that the same companies reported in the last 12 months. On the other hand, the remaining 493 companies reported earnings growth of 11.9% in Q3, and this is the second consecutive quarter of double-digit growth in the last three years. Therefore, I remain confident with the S&P 500 in 2026, but I believe there will be a change in the mix of contributors.

In my opinion, 2026 will mark a robust improvement in the results of the other 493 companies compared to the Magnificent Seven, driven by the maturation of artificial intelligence projects and greater bargaining power with suppliers, which will bring gains in productivity.

This thesis actually makes perfect sense, since we analyzed above that the market has better expectations for results in sectors such as healthcare, financials, and others. Now, let's look at my target estimate for the S&P 500 by the end of 2026.

Target the S&P 500 in 2026

To estimate a target for the S&P 500 by the end of 2026, I will use three methods supported by statistical references. The first reference looks at the number of times the S&P 500 fell more than 15% in the year, recovered, and delivered a return above double digits.

The reason for using this reference is that this occurred this year when post-Liberation Day proved to be an excellent buying point. This pattern has occurred four times since 1950, and on average, the return has been 19% after 12 months. If we use 19% upside to the current level of 6829 points, we have a target of 8126 points at the end of 2026.

Another reference studied how many times the market delivered 7 positive months in a row in the S&P 500, as we witnessed at the end of November. We can see in the table below that this occurred 16 times, and on average, the return was 9.2% after 12 months. In this scenario, we would have a target of 7457 points by the end of 2026.

Finally, the last reference studied what happened with the average return of the S&P 500 when interest rates were cut with the market close to ATH. Therefore, if we consider that the market will remain at ATH until the FOMC meeting next week, we have an average return of 13.9% after 12 months. In this scenario, the target for the S&P 500 would be 7778 points.

If we average the three methodologies to estimate a target for the S&P 500 at the end of 2026, we have a target of 7787 points and an upside of 14%, which corroborates my recommendation to buy assets that track the S&P 500 index. Additionally, I feel confident with the recommendation when I see that the Quant tools of Seeking Alpha also indicate purchase.

Potential Threats to the Bullish Thesis

There are several risks to the thesis, and they are very clear in my opinion. The Fed is carrying out a process of cutting interest rates, and although it is necessary to stimulate the job market, inflation remains outside the target. If inflation gets out of control, the Fed will have to raise interest rates again, and this could have a negative impact on the stock market.

Still regarding the Fed and FOMC, President Trump is expected to nominate Jerome Powell's replacement in the coming weeks. The impact of this indication is binary in my opinion; either it will indicate to the investor that the FED's independence is under threat, or it will not. If independence is threatened, we may see a drop in prices, but I will soon delve deeper into this topic in a specific article.

There are also risks if the Trump administration sends checks to Americans due to the tariffs. The market begins to price that this is possible, and although it should accelerate consumption, it could negatively impact inflation.

Another curious risk is that although the S&P 500 is rising, American consumer sentiment is declining. Could we see a correction in prices to match consumer sentiment?

Finally, more than 30% of the S&P 500's return for the year is concentrated in Alphabet, Nvidia, and Microsoft, meaning there is a great risk of concentration. If the competition mentioned in this article has a worse impact than the market expects, prices could fall sharply.

The Bottom Line

I believe that 2026 will mark the beginning of natural selection in AI, especially among the Magnificent Seven, which invest the most in the segment and are surrounded by expectations. Does this indicate that you should not invest in these companies? No, but your analysis must be even more cautious.

On the other hand, I see many opportunities in the S&P 500 ex-Magnificent Seven, which should benefit from the greater offer of services and products in AI, and have few expectations regarding its results, which are improving by the way.

As for my recommended stock portfolio, it already portrays this feeling in parts; however, I would say that adjustments should include a subtle reduction in exposure to the Magnificent Seven. However, I will create my own article to explain this in the future.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate the recommendation to buy assets that track the S&P 500. Although 2026 is expected to bring new challenges, there are clear opportunities. The fact is that target estimates indicate an excellent risk-return ratio.

data.title: Amazon's AWS Brings AI Customization Capabilities To Enterprise
data.description:
One of the biggest challenges in creating a successful AI deployment within enterprise companies today is fully tapping into the unique requirements, data sets and existing infrastructure that every organization has. I’d argue that every new AI solution sounds like a great option if you’re a startup and don’t have an existing environment into which you need to integrate these new tools. But existing companies all have to deal with these issues.

That’s why I’m intrigued by several of the new announcements that Amazon’s (AMZN) AWS cloud computing division made at their annual re:Invent conference here in Las Vegas. While other news from the event may get more initial attention, the company’s new AWS Factories and Nova Forge AI model customization tool look to bring some critically needed new capabilities to the many, many organizations which are still trying to implement AI in a clearly beneficial, meaningful way. Toss in some important control and evaluation tools for creating and deploying agents - which most companies need to fully trust the work agents can do before they start using them in their environments - and you have a trifecta of AI features targeted to meet the customization needs of existing enterprises.

Arguably the most interesting announcement is the news on AWS Factories. What was interesting, though, is that in some ways the company downplayed it as just extending a service they already offer to a tiny set of customers to a wider audience. AWS Factories enables organizations to set up their own AWS-powered AI infrastructure in the on-prem environment of the customer. Companies can choose to install AI infrastructure racks powered either by AWS Trainium AI accelerators or the latest Nvidia GPUs as well as the full AWS AI software stack. Practically speaking, this offers an important new degree of flexibility that companies in highly regulated industries can immediately benefit from.

Philosophically speaking, however, the news is much bigger because it highlights AWS’s willingness to extend their custom AI environment in ways that even just last year the company said it had no intention of doing. Of course, market realities have kicked in, and AWS smartly realized that the desire for on-prem AI workloads is extremely common (as my recent study “The Future of AI is Hybrid” found to be the case) - even beyond regulated industries. In addition, the other major cloud and AI model providers have all now created an option for running models on-premises, so you could argue that AWS is a bit late to the game. In truth, though, the move toward on-prem and Hybrid AI models that leverage both local datacenter and public cloud-based resources has just started. As a result, Amazon’s move is early enough that they won’t miss out on what could prove to be a very large opportunity. Plus, by allowing individual companies to get access to AWS custom silicon for their own on-prem workloads, they’ve even outpaced Google (GOOG, GOOGL), who just announced the intention to sell its custom TPU AI accelerators to third parties.

What makes the AWS Factories news even more interesting is that Amazon also announced a new effort to work first with Google Cloud (and next year with Microsoft’s (MSFT) Azure) to ease the process of creating multi-cloud environments. For decades, “multi-cloud” was a dirty word inside the halls of AWS, and the company was relatively slow to enable on-prem hybrid cloud offerings when that phenomenon started to hit. So, to see Amazon simplifying the creation of a hybrid, multi-cloud and hybrid AI world is pretty amazing.

The company’s new Nova Forge offering is interesting on many levels as well. First, it builds on the fact that, after a few initial stumbles with their own models, Amazon has continued to build out its Nova family of foundation models (several new versions were also introduced at the show) and emphasized the company’s commitment to these efforts. But more importantly, Nova Forge represents an intriguing new way for companies to leverage their own data to create customized AI models specifically trained to their unique needs. Instead of just fine-tuning an existing model, as most companies have tried, Nova Forge provides a means to fully train a custom frontier model without the enormous costs and complexity of trying to create a new model from scratch. Essentially, Nova Forge allows you to insert your custom data into several different (and early) stages of the training process via various pre-written “recipes”, and in that way, adjust the open weights of the model. The net result goes way beyond the normal RAG-style fine-tuning techniques and allows for advanced capabilities such as reinforcement learning as the model continues to be used.

Of course, we’re not just in an AI but an agentic AI world these days, so AWS also had a number of big announcements related to their agent development tools. One of the most important is an extension to the company’s existing Bedrock AgentCore framework for building and deploying agents. The new AgentCore Evaluations will constantly monitor the actions of any agents to basically ensure that they are doing what they’re supposed to be doing and not doing what they’re supposed to avoid. Along with a new security-focused extension for AgentCore, these new features should give companies more confidence that the billions of agents that Amazon (and many other tech vendors) believe will soon be running in corporate environments can be trusted. Plus, building on the customization theme, the Evaluations for agents can be customized so that the quality of the output/actions they generate can be made to match the specific requirements of each organization.

In addition to these customization-focused AI capabilities, there were - as always - a wealth of other big announcements the company made at re:Invent. On the silicon side, the formal release of their Trainium 3 accelerator, a new rack design for the Trainium 3 with Amazon’s custom chip-to-chip, rack-to-rack, and datacenter-to-datacenter networking capabilities and even a quick tease of Trainium 4 all highlighted that Amazon continues to be a big believer in the critical important of custom silicon. The company also unveiled new fully autonomous “frontier agents” designed to help dramatically improve the productivity of software developers and much more.

All told, it was yet another firehose of new announcements and yet another example of how quickly developments in AI and agents continue to occur. Most importantly, though, at a bigger-picture level, it highlighted a new kind of attitude from AWS. Several of the announcements made it clear that they see and understand their role as part of a much bigger tech ecosystem and are working to make it easier for companies who do use products from multiple vendors (as most every company does!) to integrate AWS-powered solutions right alongside them. And that is an important step forward.

Disclaimer: Some of the author's clients are vendors in the tech industry.

Disclosure: None.

Original Source: Author

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Easterly Government Properties: Get Paid An 8% Yield While You Wait For The Stock To Rerate
data.description:
Introduction

When I last wrote about Easterly Government Properties (DEA) in September 2024, the stock was trading comfortably above current levels and the narrative felt straightforward: a high-quality portfolio of mission-critical government-leased assets, a still-reasonable balance sheet, and a dividend that looked sustainable. Fast forward fourteen months and a lot has changed, the shares have fallen roughly 27% from those levels, the quarterly dividend was cut from $0.66 to $0.45, and the market seems to have written the name off as another casualty of higher-for-longer rates and political headline risk.

Yet after digging into the Q3’25 results and the updated guidance, I still think the stock is a ‘buy’ and that the sell-off has been overdone. At just 7.3x forward FFO (well below the 11.4x peer average), an 8% yield well covered, and an improving balance sheet, the risk/reward profile as improved materially.

Recent Results

Easterly Government Properties put up a reasonable quarter with a small beat on both the top and bottom line. During the company’s Q3 results, revenues of $86.1 million were $1.2 million ahead of consensus and 15.2% higher compared to last year mostly as a result of new properties acquired. Through the company’s 2021 ATM, it issued 750k shares at $22.42, around 3% higher than current prices, to raise $16.8 million.

When looking at the company’s FFO of $0.76 per share was one cent ahead of sellside estimates and 2 cents higher compared to last year. Overall, results were impacted by higher operating expenses and higher development capex (the second of which doesn’t show up in FFO) which might explain why the market reaction following the quarter was pretty muted.

On the positive side, despite a partial government shut down that took place during the period, the company was still able to grow core FFO at the midpoint of FY’25 guidance by 3% compared to last year which I think underlying durability of the cash flows. As you might expect given the name, a significant majority of Easterly Government Properties are government tenants, mostly federal (however there’s been more of a push towards state/local and government-adjacent exposure), meaning they enter into long-term leases around 8 years or more. Other positive signals were that wholly-owned privately leased properties grew 130bps sequentially to 2.7% of ABR, with $25.32 rent per square feet (compared to $19.78psf in Q2) as it continues to diversify the tenant mix. Lastly, the company acquired land to develop a 64k square foot lab in Fort Myers, FL and a 138ksf facility in Greenwood Village, CO and the overall portfolio maintains exceptionally high occupancy rates at 97%.

On the negative side, lease expirations for 2026 have been increasing, having spiked by 2.9msf (70bps) sequentially. The company also saw expenses grow at a faster pace than the 15.2% revenue clip I mentioned earlier with property level expenses growing 19.5% compared to last year. On the conference call post quarter, management didn’t give much detail on why this was the case but the bulk of the increase landed in repairs & maintenance and utilities. Still unclear whether that’s recurring going forward but the cost inflation is still something to watch going forward.

When looking at REITs, it’s imperative that investors assess leverage and credit worthiness. On the balance sheet, the company made some progress on deleveraging with cash leverage dropping from 7.9x to 7.6x during the quarter driven primarily by a $102 million progress payment received on the FDA Atlanta development project. Management expects another step down below 7.5x once that facility is fully delivered and the lease commences in December. That’ll hopefully put them on a better bath to delivering on their medium-term target of 6x cash leverage.

Having also upsized the 2018 term loan to $200 million and added an accordion feature while KBRA reaffirmed the investment-grade rating (at BBB) with a stable outlook, I think the credit profile and the view from lenders is that the company’s balance sheet looks decent. I also appreciate management getting ahead to strengthen liquidity while rates are still relatively low. On the conference call, management also noted that with IG credit ratings now, they’re in a better position to access public bond markets which should hopefully afford them a lower (if not more competitive) cost of capital.

Outlook

Looking ahead, I think despite the share price decline from my last update on the company, there’s still reasons to be optimistic on the name. Following Q3 results, management narrowed 2025 core FFO guidance to $2.98-$3.02 which at the midpoint delivers the 3% growth over 2024 and they introduced 2026 guidance at $3.05-$3.12 which again sits squarely in the 2-3% annual growth lane they have been hammering home for the past several quarters. To me, the most telling part is that the 2026 guide only embeds $50 million of wholly-owned acquisitions and $50-$100 million of development spend yet they openly say the upper end could absorb $400 million of buys because the $1.5 billion pipeline is that deep. In my view, that is deliberate conservatism designed to rebuild credibility after years of headline risk and also after cutting the dividend.

With a weighted average remaining lease term of 8.2 years on fairly new properties with an average age of 16.4 years, I don’t really see a need to do M&A and would rather Easterly Government Properties focus more on deleveraging the balance sheet and paying a continued distribution to shareholders. On the acquisition front, we can see from the below that the dollar size of deals per year has slowed from the COVID and pre-COVID era. I think that’ll continue. While the company says that they are actively evaluating some $500 million in identified pipeline, they are likely to be very disciplined if they have their eye set on a leverage target with a 6-handle and want to work towards IG debt raises in public bond markets.

On the earnings call, management noted that with the current pipeline, they can “put a couple hundred million dollars to work so effectively with the cost of equity that’s high is a real shout out to them” and if the equity cost normalizes “we can start achieving growth rates that are even higher.” To me those lines lay it bare: management knows the assets and the pipeline can easily support 4%+ growth but they are deliberately under-promising so they can over-deliver and force the market to chase the story instead of discounting it. I believe that patient approach is exactly what this name has needed for years and the coming quarters should start proving them right.

For investors who love growth, I don’t think that a potential slowdown in acquisitions is necessarily a bad thing. It just means that Easterly Government Properties will continue to return cash to shareholders via dividend and pay down debt. Earlier in the year, Easterly Government Properties cut their dividend from $0.66 a quarter to $0.45 per quarter. Today, that’s putting a 8.3% yield on the stock at current levels and implies about a 63% payout ratio, well under the near-80% payout ratio we were at when I looked at the name back in September 2024.

While investors who’ve been following the name for a while might be disappointed by dividend cut, I ultimately think this was the right move for the company as it’ll allow much more flexibility in terms of capital allocation and continue to make progress as the company aggressively tackles debt. As the company gets closer to a 6-turn leverage profile, the implied safety of the name might help to close the valuation gap with peers.

Risks and valuation

In my view, the biggest risk remains political headline risk and the perception that federal tenancy equals vulnerability. A prolonged shutdown or unexpected restructuring of a major tenant agency could spook the market and widen the valuation discount again despite zero actual credit events in the company’s history (given the tenant profile). The second risk is execution on the leverage reduction path. If development lump-sum payments or acquisition timing slip the company could stay above 7x longer than planned and investors might lose patience. It’d also put the company’s credit profile more at risk to I’d much rather see the company reduce debt and be patient until the can return back to faster growth again.

Following the dividend cut, I think the market has become spooked on Easterly Government Properties. With 5 ‘hold’ ratings and 1 ‘sell’ rating against a single ‘buy’ rating, most analysts see more downside risk with an average price target just 6.7% above where shares trade today (source: Bloomberg).

Overall, I think the market is too bearish on Easterly Government Properties. When we look at its valuation relative to peers, we can see that the company trades at a discount at 7.3x forward FFO versus peers at 11.4x FFO. I think some of this could be due to the fact that the company has elevated levels of leverage on its balance sheet but its worth noting that the valuation discount has expanded by 1-turn since last year and provides a good safety cushion.

Bottom line

In what I generally view as an overvalued market, I think Easterly Government Properties presents a great way for investors to get yield with an undervalued asset. Today, the company has a 8.3% dividend that is now well-covered by FFO with room to spare on deleveraging towards management’s 6.0x medium term target. So far this year, the political noise and the dividend cut have pushed the stock lower to 7.3x forward FFO, a level that already bakes in far more pain than the underlying cash flows are delivering. I think that patient capital willing to look through the next few quarters of conservative guidance from management should be well rewarded with a dividend that looks sustainable based on a stable portfolio with long leases. Get paid to wait.

data.title: Valeo Looking More Interesting As Expectations Reset (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The last five years have been brutal for Valeo (OTCPK:VLEEY) (OTCPK:VLEEF) (FR.PA), as this leading French manufacturer of auto components, including critical EV and ADAS systems, has struggled with weaker overall auto production, higher costs (including tariffs), slower ramps of hybrid and EV customer programs, and under-absorbed R&D and capex spending.

While I thought Valeo was in for a rough 2025 when I last wrote about the stock, and indeed the shares are only up about 5% since then versus much better results from BorgWarner (BWA), OPmobility (OTCPK:PASTF), and Forvia (OTCPK:FAURY) (OTCPK:FURCF), I think the company is in better shape heading into 2026 than the share price reflects.

Management has shown they're serious about cost and efficiency drives, and I believe the Street is sleeping on the extent to which the company can and will benefit from the end of low-margin legacy programs, the commercial ramps of new, higher-margin programs, and the ongoing opportunities present in electrification and automation. If Valeo can manage just a bit of content-driven revenue outperformance versus underlying light vehicle volumes and generate just a bit of incremental FCF leverage, it doesn't take much to support a near-doubling of the share price from here.

Another Painful Reset to Expectations

With the recent (November 23) Capital Markets Day at Valeo and the unveiling of a new "Elevate 2028" plan, it's fair to observe that the last plan ("MoveUp") was a resounding failure even if there are some important "buts" outside of management's control.

When Valeo unveiled expectations for MoveUp in February of 2022, management was expecting around $27B in revenue for 2025. The actual number is likely to come in around 75% of that figure.

Not all of this should be blamed solely on management, though. While the pandemic wreaked havoc on auto OEMs and suppliers, the years since have hardly been easier, with issues related to component scarcity, higher overall costs, and protectionism all impacting affordability and production volumes - even now, EU volumes are still 20% below their pre-COVID levels, and the only real growth in the industry has been in China.

Where management arguably can be blamed by some is the extent to which they put the vast majority of their eggs in the electrification basket. Management was expecting 26% EV penetration in 2025 when it originally made those MoveUp projections, and actual penetration today is about half that - not so much because consumers are rejecting EVs outright, but because affordability for consumers and profitability/complexity have proven to be real issues for OEMs. Where there has been growth has been in China, where Valeo has historically been under-indexed to local OEMs (excepting Geely (OTC:GELHY OTCPK:GELYF) and over-indexed to foreign OEMs that are rapidly losing share.

With that, the new Elevate 2028 plan came in well below what the original MoveUp projections would have suggested. The "good news", such as it is, is that analysts and investors had long ago recalibrated their expectations, and, if anything, the potential conservatism in the new guidance is welcome.

Management isn't looking for much growth until 2027, with a target of basically flat revenue on what management believes will be a down market in 2026. By 2028, though, not only should underlying volumes be on the upswing, but recent awards from OEMs (particularly in China) and newer business booked at better margins should start contributing - leading to a guidance range of EUR 22B-24B (around 4% annualized growth from FY'25) for revenue and operating margins of 6% to 7% (vs. the mid-to-high 4%'s for FY'25).

Valeo management is expecting further EV penetration growth (from 20% globally in 2024 to 37%), but less is now expected from the EU and North America relative to China. Further, the company has been seeing significant growth in orders from Chinese OEMs (a recent book-to-bill of around 3x), including growth in EV powertrain and automation/safety (ADAS, interior automation, software, et al.) content.

Self-Help Seems Like A Surer Path

Margins have been a major sticking point here for years now, with the company investing significant sums to develop its EV powertrain and automation capabilities with a "build it and they will come" expectation for future order growth to leverage that spending.

Again, EV uptake has been considerably slower than expected in North America and the EU, but management is no longer looking for volume leverage to drive as much of the margin leverage. Now, there is still some expectation of that here, and given that there are EV and ADAS projects ramping up with North American and European OEMs (just later than initially hoped), I don't think that's unreasonable.

What I like, though, is that most of the margin uplift is predicted on higher margins from orders booked since 2022 (when management started turning away inadequately priced business), reduced R&D spending, and efficiencies from manufacturing and marketing. Management has increased its global sourcing (buying more machinery from Chinese providers vs. German, for instance) and likewise increased plant density - doing "more with less" and driving efficiency gains.

Electrification and Automation Core

While Valeo never went all-in on electrification the way that BorgWarner did when it spun off PHINIA (PHIN), the reality is that the increased adoption of hybrids and battery EVs is still critical to this story, as is the ongoing adoption of more sophisticated ADAS systems, interior automation, and software (up to, and including, "software-defined vehicles" that allow OEMs to reap more efficiencies by producing more standardized hardware and differentiating models with high-margin software-driven features).

Between conventional electronics like starters, alternators, and stop/start systems, and more advanced components like high-voltage chargers, inverters, and converters, traction motors, energy recovery systems, and electric superchargers, there are few meaningful electrical components in hybrid or EV powertrains where Valeo doesn't have a significant presence.

Moreover, while the notion that OEMs like General Motors (GM) would steal away the EV opportunities from companies like BorgWarner and Valeo by in-sourcing, issues with R&D, performance, cost overruns, and capital investment have largely disproven that thesis, and Valeo is well-placed as a "picks and shovels" provider to the global OEM community.

While EV adoption will remain an important part of the story, don't sleep on the ongoing adoption of more sophisticated ADAS and in-cabin systems. We're still a long way from autonomous driving, but OEMs continue to fit increasingly capable ADAS systems on new models, driving Valeo's per-car content. Likewise with in-car infotainment, condition monitoring, and so forth.

Last but not least, watch the trends in software-defined vehicles over time, as I believe this can be an important and currently underappreciated driver for Valeo. SDVs basically allow OEMs to produce fewer varieties of vehicles (more standardized platforms and components) but still offer important feature and performance differentiation through software, including infotainment, ADAS, vehicle performance, and the like. While the notion of paying a monthly subscription fee to have remote starting for your car will no doubt rankle many consumers, history strongly suggests they'll accept it in return for lower upfront costs.

The Outlook

I was expecting 2025 to be a rougher year for Valeo, and that has proven to be the case, as challenges from legacy program ends, insufficient exposure to Chinese OEMs, and delayed programs with North American and EU customers have all hit revenue (not to mention a still not-so-great passenger vehicle market).

I had been looking for a slight year-over-year growth in revenue at the start of this year, but instead Valeo is most likely going to see around a 4% reported decline. I do expect some improvement next year, though, as well as five-year growth of over 3% (which should roughly double underlying industry volume growth). Longer term, if content growth can drive just a few tenths of a point of incremental growth over underlying volumes (and content in software-defined vehicles is almost 10x legacy vehicles, while EV content can be 50% higher or more), Valeo should generate 2%-plus revenue growth.

On the margin side, I do expect EBITDA margins to improve to around 14% for this year and then slowly improve toward the mid-teens thereafter while operating margin moves from the 4%s toward 6%. Even if I take a more conservative approach, though, and model only about 50 bp of free cash flow margin improvement over the next decade (a 30 bp uplift for 2025-2034 relative to 2015-2024), that's enough to support around 7% annualized FCF growth and a near-doubling of the share price.

So too with a multiples-based approach. Valeo has long traded for well below what its margins would normally suggest as "fair" (based on historical relationships in the sector), and while the many disappointments support an argument that there should be some discount, today's discount seems extreme. The shares currently trade around 0.33x forward revenue, while even an extreme bearish outlook would typically support something more on the order of 0.4x to 0.5x (roughly 50% to 100% upside).

The Bottom Line

Valeo's performance over the last five years has been painful at best, but I do think there are still significant fundamental drivers in play here - including strong positions in EV powertrain and automation-enabling components. With electrification adoption expectations washed out and reset, and ongoing growth in areas like ADAS and in-cabin automation, I do think Valeo can do at least a bit better than underlying volumes over the coming years, and with better margins.

This remains a high-risk call that obviously hasn't worked out in some time, but I think expectations are at a level now where this is worth a closer look.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CTO Realty Growth: Buy A High-Quality REIT Offering 8.4% Yield
data.description:
Thesis

A Company that deserves a "BUY" recommendation, according to my view, is CTO Realty Growth (NYSE:CTO).

It is a stock more suited to patient income investors who are comfortable with the volatility of retail real estate.

It combines interesting lease dynamics, visible organic growth through signed-not-open leases (SNO, i.e. signed leases that have not yet started generating income) and active capital management with share repurchases and property recycling.

Recent quarterly results are not “explosive” but they build a foundation for improvement in 2026 and 2027.

The increase in core FFO (Funds From Operations, operating profits before depreciation and amortization) in absolute numbers, the advanced re-leasing of large anchor boxes and the SNO pipeline of $ 5.5 million in annual rents create a fairly clear path to higher recurring revenues.

At the same time, leverage remains elevated, with net debt to EBITDA at 6.7x.CTO looks more like a “buy under pressure” opportunity than a yield trap. This depends on whether the management will continue to execute on its SNO, anchors and deleveraging plan.

As I see it, the long term and stable character of the investment is seen on the board as the stock is gaining 52% the last decade.

This return comes from dividends as the stock price remains unchanged.

Leasing Momentum and Portfolio Upgrade

This return comes from dividends as the stock price remains unchanged. On the operational front, the picture is clearly positive.

Since the beginning of the year until the end of December, the company completed 482,000 square feet of total leasing activity, 424,000 square feet of those were comparable leases with an average base rent increase of 21.7%.

For a shopping center portfolio, this indicates a strong negotiating position in individual local markets.

In Q3 143,000 square feet of new leases, renewals and expansions were signed with an average base rent of $23 per square foot.

Of the 125,000 comparable square feet, rents increased by 10.3%.

The company is not filling gaps with discounting but is improving the average rent per square foot. Same-property NOI rose by 2.3%.

The rate is not impressive by itself but it reflects:

• replacement of old tenants with more productive uses, such as in Beaver Creek, where the old movie theater was replaced by a Onelife Fitness gym and

• strong performance of small stores in West Broad Village, Plaza at Rockwall and Ashford Lane.

The Management’s approach is also worth noting: in several cases (particularly at Shops at Legacy) potential tenants with weak financials or excessive demands for tenant improvements were rejected.

This shows that the goal is not “any” occupancy but quality leasing that will support long-term NOI and property value.

SNO Pipeline and Anchor Box Re-Leasing

At the center of the investment case is the re-leasing of the ten major anchor boxes.

Six have already been leased while the remaining four are in advanced negotiations.

So far, the signed contracts correspond to approximately $2.5 million in new annual rents.

The potential total contribution from the ten anchors is $4 to $4.5 million, if management's goals are achieved.

The goal of a positive cash leasing spread of 40% to 60% across these ten anchors is ambitious but not unrealistic especially in markets where the previous leases were tenant-friendly.

If these plans be realized it will be a significant driver of NOI growth itself, not just a gradual improvement in occupancy.

At the same time, the signed-not-open pipeline amounts to $5.5 million in annual rents.

This corresponds to approximately 5.3% of the portfolio's existing cash base rents.

The CFO was quite specific about the timeline:

• approximately 75% of the amount (around $4 million) is expected to be recognized in 2026,

• the integration will be gradual, from approximately $0.5 million in the first quarter to approximately $1.5 million in the fourth,

• by the end of 2026, all tenants currently in the SNO pipeline will have opened and are paying regular cash rent, with a full impact of $5.5 million in 2027.

Shops at Legacy is a clear example of a value-add opportunity.

The former WeWork space has essentially been re-leased into two new uses:

• a 20,000 square foot private members' club,

• a 30,000 square foot coworking space, scheduled to open by the end of 2026.

Along with approximately 60,000 square meters of small stores (catering, gyms, retail) signed in the last two years, the center's occupancy rate has risen to close to 85%.

The remaining vacancy is mainly smaller surfaces where the company states that it is consciously selective.

The Company is also planning to buy a new commercial center in South Florida.

Balance Sheet, Capital Allocation and Share Repurchases

The Company took advantage of the interest rate stabilization window to improve its debt profile.

Before the end of the quarter, two debt transactions totaling $150 million were completed:

• a new five-year $125 million loan maturing in September 2030,

• a $25 million increase in an existing loan maturing in September 2029.

Both loans carry a SOFR interest rate plus a margin depending on leverage.

Thanks to interest rate swap agreements already in place, the effective interest rate starts at around 4.2%.

It is also expected to adjust to around 4.7% from 2026, based on the current balance sheet structure.

The proceeds were used to repay a $65 million loan maturing in 2026 and to reduce the balance on the credit line.

After these moves, the Company has approximately $170 million in total liquidity ($161 million available in the revolving credit facility and $9 million in cash).

It is noted that only $17.8 million of loans mature in 2026, which significantly reduces short-term financial risk.

The net debt to EBITDA ratio is at 6.7x, slightly improved from 6.9x in the previous quarter.

The most important is that only the integration of SNO leases is estimated to be able to remove part of the leverage.

This does not take into account possible sales of mature properties (asset recycling).

On the shareholder side, management is demonstrating in practice that it considers the valuation attractive:

• repurchases of $9.3 million at an average price of $16.27 per share,

• activation of a new $10 million repurchase program after the completion of the previous one.

The CEO even called current price levels “almost ridiculous” with a P/FFO of under 9x and a dividend yield of close to 10%.

The company also has the ability to recycle capital by selling stabilized assets at lower cap rates and redeploying proceeds into higher-yield opportunities or share repurchases.

As i see it this capital allocation strategy supports the long-term investment case.

Risks

CTO Realty Growth has its own dangers despite its positive signs.

First of all leverage is high. Manageable but high.

Net debt to EBITDA is around 6.7x, which does not leave much room if things go off plan.

Especially if there are delays in new leases opening or in filling the 40,000-square-foot anchor at Carolina Pavilion would make it harder to reduce debt quickly.

If the demand in specific local markets slows down then the reduction of debt will take longer time.

Second, the business model is clearly exposed to retail risk.

The assets are mainly open-air malls with more “durable” uses (supermarkets, fitness, catering, services).

Therefore, the sector is sensitive to consumption slowdown or to changes to the preferences of the tenants.

Additionally, the dependence on large anchors means that a potential departure of an important tenant could affect negatively the center's profitability.

Third, the value-add strategy requires significant expenditures for tenant improvements and leasing commissions.

As already mentioned, space development subsidies were very low in the first half of the year and increased in the third quarter.

They are expected to remain elevated in the Q4 as new anchor leases are delivered.

If these costs exceed initial estimates or if tenants do not perform as expected, the return on invested capital will decline.

Fourth, the investment case is largely based on the success of the SNO pipeline and the upgrade of the ten anchors.

This is essentially a concentrated set of movements.

Any slippage in the schedule, problems with a specific tenant (e.g. in the co-working or the private members club) or turbulence in local markets could turn the current “visible growth” into a more uncertain scenario.

Finally, valuation risk should not be underestimated.

The risk relates to the possibility that the market will decide to permanently price retail REITs at lower multiples. And this is critical regardless of FFO improvement.

And so, the stock may remain “cheap” for longer than an impatient investor would like.

Conclusion

CTO Realty Growth is in an in-between phase: it is neither a “deep value” story in distress nor a “growth” REIT with impressive growth rates.

It is, more, a case of stable commercial real estate with a specific plan to increase revenues the next two years.

The plan is based on four points:

• strong performance in new leases and positive rent increases on comparable leases,

• a substantial SNO pipeline of $ 5.5 million expected to feed into 2026 and 2027,

• restructuring of ten large anchor boxes with the aim of significantly increasing rents,

• disciplined balance sheet management and aggressive share repurchases at prices that management itself considers particularly low.

For an investor looking for a combination of high dividend yield and visible, though gradual, FFO growth over a 2–3 year horizon, CTO could be an attractive position.

The key is being comfortable with retail exposure and higher leverage. If the company achieves its goals in leasing and deleveraging, earnings and valuation could improve.

As far as I am concerned the stock is an undervalued opportunity instead of a value trap and is suitable for those that can wait and are not affected by the negative sentiment in the retail real estate.

data.title: Upstart: Improving Fundamentals And A Still Discounted Price
data.description:
Introduction:

Upstart (UPST) has been able to continue executing its vision of using AI to help automate consumer lending and the most recent quarterly results showed significant progress across the board.

The business has expanded its ability to underwrite loans and verify potential borrowers while also securing long term funding commitments from its partners which should stabilize the business even if economic conditions deteriorate over the coming years.

Given the stock's decline over the past year, shares are trading at compelling forward multiples and therefore I am rating Upstart a BUY.

Business Description & Key Considerations:

Upstart is a company that helps reduce the cost and complexity of borrowing money through the use of advanced AI systems. The firm's models have been trained over 98 million borrower repayment events, and they are reportedly excellent at considering the risk of lending to individual consumers while taking into account macroeconomic factors as well.

Upstart's platform currently supports personal, auto, and home equity loans, and the firm's long term vision is to offer even more options for users over time. The company generates revenue from both platform fees, where lenders pay Upstart to use its AI lending marketplace, and referral fees where partners are charged for each loan originated through the platform.

Throughout FY25, 91% of the loans originated through Upstart were entirely automated, and around 87% of these loans were funded by institutional investors and Upstart's bank lending partners. Also, around 50% of funding currently comes from committed arrangements with investors where they specify the amount of risk they're willing to take on along with the returns they expect.

Securing commitments is incredibly important as they provide stability when credit markets tighten since Upstart's partners are contractually obligated to purchase loans that meet the agreed upon criteria. It should be noted that the number of loans completed entirely using AI automation has stagnated compared to the year ago period, which indicates to me that the firm's internal models may have reached a point where achieving further improvements will be difficult and take some time to materialize.

While Upstart's business is focused on underwriting loans, the company also uses its AI models for things like income and identity verification, and to detect if potential borrowers have already taken out multiple loans from many different lenders simultaneously.

Upstart has been able to substantially improve the speed of its personal loan pricing engine recently, which allows the firm to now run larger and more complex underwriting models that have a higher number of parameters and data inputs.

Similarly, Upstart has created a new machine learning model to ensure its take rates remain in the "goldilocks zone" so to speak (not too high, and not too low). For certain borrowers, Upstart can raise take rates without hurting conversions, and in other instances they must lower fees in order to remain competitive.

Furthermore, the company has introduced a new method related to marketing, which has worked so far and led to a 50% increase in originations at the same level of marketing expenditures.

While Upstart did achieve a number of improvements across its tech stack, its AI models likely misinterpreted some macroeconomic indicators during the quarter which led to the company reducing the number of loan approvals it granted and increasing interest rates for the borrowers it did approve.

This may've left a sour taste in many users' mouths as numerous individuals with solid credit were likely unable to secure the financing they desired through the platform. While the models have since corrected themselves (at least according to management), this event underscores my view that the largest risk to Upstart's business is reputational in nature.

If this continues to happen on a routine basis, people will simply stop using the platform to get loans and will go to one of the many alternative credit sources that exist instead.

Shifting gears, Upstart has made significant progress in streamlining the application process for consumers who wish to open a home equity line of credit (HELOC), as automatic approvals have grown from 1% back in June of this year to 20% by the end of October.

If this metric continues on this trajectory, the company will likely see an uptick in the number of consumers applying for HELOCs through the platform given the ease and simplicity of taking out a loan through the platform relative to many other traditional mortgage lenders.

Finally, right after the release of the most recent quarterly earnings results, Upstart inked a one year forward-flow agreement with Castlelake. Under this deal, the alternative investment firm is set to buy up to $1.5 billion worth of consumer loans that will be originated through Upstart's platform.

While this does introduce a degree of customer concentration risk for the company given the size of the deal, the two have successfully worked together numerous times in the past and there is nothing to indicate that this agreement will work out any differently.

As a matter of fact, I believe that this announcement should act as a strong tailwind as it shows that Upstart has been able to consistently deliver on its promises to this specific group of sophisticated investors to help them identify strong borrower candidates and earn reasonable rates of return.

Financials & Valuation:

Upstart reported relatively strong third quarter results, with transaction volume in dollars rising 80% YoY to ~$2.85 billion, whereas the number of loans approved during the quarter increased 128% YoY to 428K. The significantly faster growth rate in the number of loans approved reflects lower loan sizes as the firm's AI models uncovered many low risk borrowers who applied for small loans.

Upstart's conversion rate, which effectively measures how many borrowers that signaled interest in taking out a loan through the platform ended up getting funded, increased 430bps YoY to 20.6%. However, management noted that the company's models actually reduced the number of approvals sequentially due to higher macroeconomic risk.

Upstart's total revenue increased 70.9% YoY to $277.1 million in Q3 FY25, with net fee revenue growing 54.3% YoY to $258.5 million. Drilling deeper, platform and referral fees increased by around 61.6% YoY to $216.9 million, and servicing fees rose 24.8% YoY to $41.7 million.

Net interest income (which includes adjustments to the fair value of loan securities held on Upstart's balance sheet) came in at $18.6 million, which is a sharp turnaround compared to the $5.5 million loss incurred during the year ago period.

While there were a lot of positives this past quarter, the firm's variable expenses tied to its loan origination business (including borrower acquisition, verification, and servicing costs) increased 70.4% YoY to $111.1 million. This led to the company's contribution margin declining considerably from 61% a year ago down to just 57%.

Finally, Upstart's total operating expenses increased 22.3% YoY to $253.4 million, demonstrating substantial operating leverage. This allowed the firm to post a positive operating income balance, coming in at $23.7 million compared to a $45.2 million loss in Q3 FY24. Similarly, Upstart generated a net profit of nearly $32 million this most recent quarter, compared to a $6.7 million loss last year.

Having gone over the financial results, let's now take a look at Upstart's current valuation. The stock trades at a modest 18.7x FY26 earnings, which is largely a result of the sell-off over the past year (with shares declining by around 34% over that time frame).

I personally believe that this presents a buying opportunity, as the company is expected to benefit from both high revenue growth and operating leverage over the next two years (with EPS expected to expand more rapidly than the top line).

Furthermore, Upstart's vision to begin offering more borrowing options to users should increase engagement on the platform and boost both transaction volume and the total number of loan approvals.

Conclusion:

Upstart's third quarter print demonstrated tangible momentum in terms of originations and operating profitability, which is a major positive in my view. The company's ability to continue iterating and improving its AI models, while also securing new partnerships and helping its borrower base signal immense operational strength.

While the firm's reputational risk and sensitivity to the macro economy should remain top of mind, I believe that the fundamentals of the business will keep improving and the relatively low valuation multiples justify a BUY rating on the stock.

data.title: VIOV: Continued Underperformance Is Likely
data.description:
The Vanguard S&P Small-Cap 600 Value Index Fund ETF Shares (VIOV), launched on 09/07/2010 and managed by The Vanguard Group, Inc., provides exposure to the small-cap segment of the equity market filtered based on value characteristics.

In this article, I am going to go over the portfolio, the recent performance of this ETF, and the macro conditions that shape the outlook for it. Although this fund is well-diversified and the value factor makes it defensive in times of heightened uncertainty like this, there are implications that point to continued underperformance in at least the near term.

Portfolio and Performance

The portfolio is as diversified as you'd expect from a small-cap ETF, with the most meaningful exposure being represented by Sandisk (SNDK), a spinoff company that experienced its stock price increase by more than 500% in 2025. Aside from that, the weights appear to be very conservative, with the rest of the top holdings accounting for 0.8-1% of the portfolio each.

The sector distribution is equally modest, with tech stocks accounting for only ~17% of the portfolio, a far cry from the current exposure of the large-cap segment. As value stocks are most often found in bank stocks using traditional valuation metrics as criteria, financial stocks make up the majority of the portfolio at ~19%.

Still, consumer cyclical stocks are a meaningful part of the ETF and make VIOV highly sensitive to consumer sentiment and, therefore, the macro backdrop. Speaking of which, from the YTD performance relative to the S&P 500, it's evident that the market felt even less comfortable with small caps (even value ones) back in April. YTD, VIOV has underperformed the broader market by a meaningful margin.

This trend is unlikely to change based on what we know about the macroeconomic conditions.

Opportunities and Risks

Inflation Expectations

Recent inflation readings, such as CPI, put inflation around 3%. Core CPI is now at the same level, hinting at a structural reflation trend.

Consumers expect inflation to keep rising, and that is fairly consistent with the current price pressures implied by higher tariffs. With the most likely scenario being that companies will keep exhausting their pre-tariff inventories, we might actually see more than a 3.2% rate in the near term; Gregory Daco from EY-Parthenon expects that tariffs are going to result in a 0.8 percentage point increase by early 2026.

With small caps being more sensitive to inflationary pressures than large caps, the implication is that VIOV constituents will feel the impact of raw-material costs more immediately. This translates into margin pressures, as the recent lag between tariff implementation and the price increases indicates both front-loading and cost absorption due to uncertainty about the consumer's appetite.

Labor Market Conditions

The labor market, on the other hand, acts as a brake for inflation growth to some extent because wage inflation pressures are supposed to be modest, given the rapid job growth deceleration the economy experienced recently. Nonfarm payroll growth has fallen from well above 1% early this year to just 0.83% lately.

Additionally, unemployment has risen to 4.4%, according to the September jobs report, and the number of claims for unemployment insurance remaining high implies further increases ahead.

Actually, Global Investment Research estimates unemployment to be as high as 4.5% by the end of the year, which doesn't seem so far-fetched anymore.

Because many of VIOV's holdings don't have a lot of flexibility when it comes to labor costs, which are usually significant, a softening labor market can be seen as a relative advantage for this ETF. On the other hand, rising unemployment implies weaker spending, which is a risk for VIOV. Currently, the labor market risks outweigh the opportunities. Though inflation is not expected to rise aggressively, the same level of confidence is not often exhibited by analysts when it comes to the labor market.

Monetary Policy Path

Some offsetting is provided by the monetary policy path, however, which is probably going to lean towards accelerated easing and not the same pace of rate cuts we have seen up to this point. That's because the labor market represents a greater risk than inflation, as we've seen above.

The Fed lowered the federal funds rate by 25 bps in the last meeting, and the overwhelming majority of bets imply that the market largely expects it to cut by another 25 bps in nine days. This is consistent with the current balance of risks and the reasoning that amid a government shutdown and delayed reports, the safest thing to do is to keep easing.

With lower borrowing costs, small caps are expected to benefit directly since they have much less financing cost flexibility than large caps. At the same time, lower rates will translate into higher valuation multiples.

Growth Trends

With the US economy entering a softer growth phase, the main risk for small caps is related to an opportunity cost. Real GDP growth averaged 1.4% in the first half of this year, and economic forecasts point to a rate that is below historical averages for the next year too.

Value is a defensive factor, but in a scenario where the economy slows down, the cyclical nature of small caps will prove a meaningful headwind for VIOV. Value is outweighed by the small-cap factor and is unlikely to help the ETF to perform equally or better than mid-cap and large-cap peers. The small-cap factor is aggressive and does better when the pace of growth picks up.

Conclusion

Therefore, I am rating VIOV a hold. I am a big fan of value investing, and small caps are the segment where you can find good opportunities. But this ETF is not actively managed, and the outlook implies further underperformance for it.

Now, I am always open to challenging my views because being an ideologue in investing and trading can prove expensive. So if you have a different opinion, this platform is the right place to discuss both strategy and the various options with which we can position our portfolios. Go ahead and leave a comment below to let me know what you think.

Otherwise, please let me know if you found this article useful. It means a lot!

Thank you for reading.

data.title: The Predictability Premium: Why Clearway Energy Wins
data.description:
Elevator Thesis

Predictability and growth are the two attributes that are surprisingly rare in the Renewable Electricity industry. Interestingly, Clearway Energy, Inc. (CWEN) (CWEN.A) is delivering this two-punch combo.

Clearway purchases long-term clean-energy assets yielding reliable cash instead of pursuing large, risky development bets.

This proves to be a smart strategy when we look at the company’s recent outcomes. For perspective, the cash flow is increasing, operations are picking up, and new projects are coming in at a normal pace.

Surely the debt is high, but the broader picture is heading in the right direction. Clearway is acquiring assets that actually pay back, and this instills confidence for the coming years.

Furthermore, the company has a strong dividend and its valuation remains reasonable in comparison to its peers.

Spoiler alert: Clearway is a buy right now!

Clearway Q3 '25 Earnings

Clearway’s Q3 was not perfect, but the key trends are encouraging. The net income increased to $60 million as compared to $27 million last year. Lower taxes were helpful, but the actual stimulus was operating efficiency. Adjusted EBITDA increased to $385 million from $354 million, even as revenue declined by 11.7% to $429 million. This means that Clearway is working to convert current assets into cash instead of pursuing top-line growth.

Moreover, CAFD also rose to $166 million compared to $146 million a year ago, despite the higher-rate environment. This points to disciplined operations and strong asset performance. Flexible Generation availability increased to 92.5% from 87.5% due to low outages. Meanwhile, Renewables and Storage output increased to 5,151 thousand MWh compared to 4,955 thousand MWh as new facilities became available and wind conditions improved.

These findings made management comfortable to reduce the 2025 CAFD guidance to $420-$440 million, indicating that the company is stabilizing while still growing.

That said, this growth push is draining the balance sheet. The liquidity has decreased to $834 million versus $1.33 billion at year’s end. Whereas, cash (TTM) is around $251 million versus total debt of $9.2 billion. Hence, the cash/debt ratio is extremely low and the net leverage remains high.

Growth That Pays Off

Clearway is quietly carving a noticeable path for growth. For example, the management emphasized more than 2 GW of opportunities for 2026-2027, with a solid and concrete pipeline.

Additionally, Clearway relies on repowerings and drop-downs in order to grow and remain flexible. Projects like San Juan Mesa prolong the asset life and enhance their production, which directly enhances CAFD, particularly in wind-heavy areas.

Furthermore, the 613 MW of solar acquisition in October will add additional predictable cash flow across eight states with approximately ten-year contracts. Clearway’s net investment of $210-230 million is undoubtedly big, however, risk remains in check since the portfolio is completely contracted.

Altogether, these moves make the company's growth evident and low-risk. The money from new projects will sustain the increasing financial needs. In the meantime, management’s 2030 targets give a clear long-term direction and a reason to stay confident.

Now, the question revolves around leverage. Will cash flow from new projects settle increasing debt costs? The answer depends on disciplined capital placement and more emphasis on investments that maximize returns. If Clearway stays focused, such growth initiatives will not only increase the asset base but will also increase CAFD and provide sustainable dividends.

A High-Yield Edge

Clearway’s dividend stands out as a core strength for income investors. The yield is sustainable and represents discipline in capital allocation at $1.81 annually, with a payout ratio of approximately 74%.

Both share classes have a slight difference in forward yields. CWEN.A currently has a 5.35% forward yield compared to 5.01% of CWEN which is a slight discount. This makes CWEN.A a little more appealing to yield-oriented investors despite reduced liquidity.

A five-year dividend growth rate of 13.28% is an indicator of structural resilience. In contrast to businesses that pursue rapid growth, Clearway takes advantage of long-term contracts to grow cash returns gradually. This reliability provides assurance that payouts will continue even amid market volatility.

The differences between the share classes influence the investor’s choice. The liquidity of CWEN (trading over 1 million shares daily) is high enough to cater to institutional investors who require flexibility and large positions. CWEN.A (trading a few hundred thousand shares per day) is light-volume and is more appealing to retail investors or long-term income investors. Both provide the same economic exposure, but with a slightly higher yield, CWEN.A presents an incremental upside for investors who are ready to have lower liquidity in exchange for increased cash flow.

Valuation Snapshot

Clearway's valuation is both promising and cautious.

The stock is appealing on a trailing basis. The GAAP P/E (TTM) is at 14.6x, approximately 29% under the sector median of 20.5x, showing that investors are not overpaying on recent profitability. Its P/CF (TTM) at 5.8x stands better than the sector's 9x, offering a 35.5% discount. The company is generating cash effectively as compared to its historical earnings.

However, forward expectations reflect expected pressure on future earnings. CWEN.A is trading at a forward P/E of 42x, which is over two times the sector's 20.5x, while CWEN clocks in at 34.9x. These numbers fail to reflect the cash strength of the company. Non-cash elements of GAAP earnings have the effect of lowering reported earnings, even though cash flow and dividends are strong. This will help the company to sustain payouts and cope with short-term challenges.

Peer Comparison: I chose NextEra Energy( NEE) and Brookfield Renewable( BEP)( BEPC) to do a comparative analysis since these are the largest U.S. yieldcos and offer a benchmark for valuation and performance.

CWEN’s GAAP P/E is significantly lower than NEE’s high-20s range and far below Brookfield's 96x, whose low earnings base overstates the multiple. The PEG ratio of about 0.12x further proves that the stock appears cheap compared to projected growth. Clearway is evidently providing the best value on a pure equity basis.

Furthermore, enterprise metrics offer more insight. Forward EV/Sales at 12x is slightly higher than NextEra but lower than Brookfield. Indeed, the market is paying a modest premium for revenue.
