However, EV/EBITDA of 13.8x is less than that of NextEra's 16.4x and much less than Brookfield's 28.7x, indicating efficient operating profitability.

All in all, Clearway provides reasonable valuation, high cash generation, and stable income.

Fair Value Range

Currently, Clearway trades around $34, up about 23% YOY.

I valued Clearway using a DCF model. The model begins with the 2024 FCF of approximately $483 million and projects it forward over the next five years with varying growth projections. Each scenario implies a discount rate that matches the risk profile. I then subtract approximately $8.9 billion of net debt after computing the present value of these cash flows and the terminal value.

Bear case: Considering that the projects would progress slowly, free cash flow only grows at 1% a year, and the WACC is 7 - 7.5%. This provides a value of $26-$27 per share, or around 21% lower than $34. Most cash flows are contracted, so the downside is limited.

Base case: It assumes constant project execution, with 3% annual free cash flow growth, at a WACC of 6%. The model provides $47-$48 per share or about 40% above $34. It's a balance of pipeline growth, contracted cash flow, and reasonable financing costs; therefore, it’s the most likely scenario.

Bull Case: It uses a higher free cash flow growth of 5% a year, a WACC between 4.5 and 5%, and smooth execution. The value amounts to $71-$73 per share, which is more than double the current price.

A probability-weighted approach assuming 25% bear, 60% base, and 15% bull yields a fair value of around $46-48 per share.

At $34, Clearway trades below base-case and probability-weighted values. Project timelines, refinancing, and merchant exposure should be monitored by long-term investors who may be looking at good upside. Staggered purchasing can help to reduce risk.

Risks

To begin with, Clearway has a sizable ratio of variable-rate borrowings in its debt profile, implying that rising rates hit almost immediately. Each increase in the cost of financing consumes profits and CAFD, and unlike peers with a fixed rate structure, Clearway is less insulated. Such sensitivity can cause unstable near-term earnings.

Secondly, renewable electricity is not a “set it and forget it” business. Repowerings, maintenance, and grid upgrades are costly. Assuming that the costs remain high while Clearway proceeds with repowerings and the new 613 MW solar portfolio, the cash flows would temporarily be constrained, particularly with the already high leverage.

Finally, the strategy of Clearway is heavily based on repowering timelines, contract renewals, and the flawless integration of the drop-downs. If any of these slip, either due to permitting delays, supply chain problems, or less than expected asset output, the planned CAFD uplift can be reduced rapidly.

Moreover, since the business operates under long-term contracts, any snag with utility counterparties or changes in state policy would decrease the visibility that investors value.

Takeaway

Clearway deserves your attention.

Yes, debt is considerable, and earnings could be under pressure in the short term, but the company operates a disciplined, predictable model. It purchases cash-generating assets, carries out smart repowerings, and maintains growth without taking risky bets. Dividends are good, cash flow is getting better, and valuation remains quite reasonable relative to peers. Clearway has the potential to provide stable returns to income-oriented investors in the long run. The strategy is simple but effective. Over time, such a consistent approach is likely to be profitable. If you look beyond temporary caveats, Clearway is a reliable option.

data.title: Suncor Energy: A Value Machine With Rising Efficiency
data.description:
Suncor Energy (SU) (SU:CA), a Calgary-based integrated oilsands giant, offers a safe way to play oil’s potential upside. With robust cash flows, the company keeps returning value to investors at full speed through dividends and buybacks.

Efficiency has significantly improved since the appointment of Richard Kruger as CEO in early 2023, but the progress has not been fully priced in, and the stock still lags peers on valuation metrics.

I initiate coverage of Suncor with a ‘buy’ and a price target of $60, based on a more reasonable EV/EBITDA multiple than the market currently reflects. My target implies a potential upside of about 30% in addition to dividend income.

Quick Overview

Suncor was created in 1979 when Sun Oil, which dated back to 1917, merged its conventional oil businesses with Great Canadian Oil Sands. It has since grown into Canada’s second-biggest oilsands developer by market cap.

Upstream, the company produces more than 0.8 million barrels a day, with key assets located near the Athabasca River in Alberta. Downstream, it refines nearly half a million barrels a day in Edmonton, Montreal, Sarnia, and Commerce City, Colorado. Suncor also runs the Petro-Canada retail and wholesale distribution network.

Making Progress

Oil prices have largely declined since 2023, but Suncor’s operating cash flows have held up and formed an overall uptrend, as shown in the chart below. This is attributable to improving efficiency under the leadership of Kruger, an energy veteran and former CEO of Imperial Oil. He pushed ahead with measures to simplify the corporate structure, lay off redundant workers, cut debts, and focus on key operating metrics.

Over the last two years, the company has met or beaten its operational targets on a fairly consistent basis, including asset utilization, financial leverage, breakeven oil price, and refinery turnaround time.

In early 2024, Suncor made a three-year plan with two key goals: boost free funds flow (normalized at $75 WTI) by C$3.3 billion from about C$5 billion and reduce WTI breakeven price by $10 from $53. By the end of 2024, the company had already accomplished 70% of both tasks, and it’s well on track to meet the full targets by 2026.

Shareholder Returns

Suncor is committed to returning all its free cash flows to investors through dividends and buybacks. Since 2022, the company has distributed C$10.2 billion of dividends and repurchased C$12.5 billion of shares, according to quarterly filings through September. The combined value accounts for about 30% of Suncor’s current market cap. In the 12 months through September, dividends and buybacks added up to C$6 billion, or about 8% of the market cap.

We want to be a cash machine that invests wisely and returns capital to shareholders predictably and reliably. It's taken us a couple of years to get all the bells and whistles in place to improve fundamental performance. (CEO Kruger, Nov. 5 earnings call)

In terms of the dividend to the investors, we view that as a commitment and a promise… The cash return strategy, though, of buybacks, in our view, it is one of the best mechanisms to get cash back to our shareholders and really drive the per-share value… (Kris Smith, Executive Advisory, Nov. 5 earnings call)

A modest financial leverage helps support the company’s dividends and buybacks. Net debt currently accounts for 55% of adjusted funds from operations for the trailing 12 months, down from about 70% in 2023. The ratio of net debt to shareholders’ equity has dropped to 16% from about 23% in 2023.

Oilsands Assets

The development of oilsands usually requires a much larger upfront capital outlay than conventional oil production. However, after the initial investment, oilsands assets often enjoy the huge advantage of low natural decline and long reserve lives.

Suncor’s oilsands portfolio consists of large mines and in-situ facilities in northern Alberta with a 24-year remaining life, based on about 7 billion barrels of proved and probable reserves. The overall decline rate is minimal, with zero decline for the Fort Hills and Syncrude mines and a 10% rate for some in-situ operations.

Canada’s oilsands developers, along with US firms in the Permian Basin, have become the lowest-cost oil producers in North America, according to a report by Enverus Intelligence Research. The appeal of oilsands rises as the cost of US shale production climbs amid waning inventories. The average WTI breakeven price for new shale wells has risen to $70 and may increase further to $95 over the next 10 years, Enverus estimates. By comparison, Suncor’s breakeven has dropped below $46, with more downside projected by the company.

Facing macro weakness and US tariffs, Canada plans to spend more than C$100 billion on infrastructure over the next five years. The stimulus program, also known as ‘build, baby, build,’ is expected to include oil pipelines and transport networks that may provide a boost to oilsands developers.

Valuation

Suncor has an enterprise value of $62.5 billion as of Nov. 28, equivalent to 5.6 times estimated 2025 EBITDA, according to Seeking Alpha data. The multiple falls shy of an already cheap median of 6.4 times for the oil and gas sector in North America. As shown in the chart below, the stock looks the cheapest among big oilsands developers in Canada and lags behind some integrated oil majors in America.

With quality assets, robust shareholder returns, and rising efficiency, Suncor deserves a more reasonable valuation, at least on par with major oilsands peers in Canada. I value the stock with a forward EV/EBITDA of 7.2 times, based on the average multiple between Imperial Oil, Canadian Natural Resources, and Cenovus Energy. This ratio, combined with an estimated 11.2 billion EBITDA, $10.4 billion debt, and $2.1 billion cash, implies a fair market cap of $72.2 billion and a price target of $60 per share.

Key Risks

I’m cautiously optimistic about oil prices over the next 12 months due to signs of tight supply and AI-driven demand for energy. However, if WTI drops significantly from current levels, Suncor will probably face a margin squeeze and lower free funds flow, which in turn will weaken shareholder returns.

The US market accounted for about 15% of Suncor’s revenue in 2024. Currently, most energy products from Canada qualify for tariff exemption under the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement, but any change to the exemption or new tariffs against Canadian oil may still affect the company’s sales.

Conclusion

Suncor has strong cash flows and commits to returning all its excess funds to shareholders. Operating metrics have consistently improved since energy veteran Richard Kruger became CEO in early 2023. Quality assets, a low breakeven oil price, and a healthy balance sheet combine to offer downside protection. Suncor looks undervalued versus Canadian oilsands peers as well as big oils in America. I initiate coverage with a ‘buy’ rating based on a more reasonable EV/EBITDA ratio than the market currently reflects.
